title: The Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost
context:
# TEMPORAL CONTEXT SNAPSHOT
## Saint John's Episcopal Cathedral, Denver, Colorado
## September 12, 2021 | Preacher: Broderick Greer

---

LITURGICAL CONTEXT

The Sixteenth Sunday after Pentecost falls within Ordinary Time in the Christian calendar. Under the Revised Common Lectionary, the readings for this date would have been Exodus 14:19-31 (the crossing of the Red Sea), Psalm 114, Romans 10:8b-13, and Matthew 10:24-39. This period of Ordinary Time emphasizes discipleship and the cost of following Christ, with Matthew's Gospel focusing on Jesus's teachings about loyalty and sacrifice.

EPISCOPAL / ANGLICAN CHURCH NEWS

The Episcopal Church was navigating the post-General Convention period following its 79th General Convention in July 2021 in Austin, Texas, where significant decisions were made regarding LGBTQ+ inclusion and reparations. The Diocese of Colorado, under Bishop William Barrera's leadership, was engaged in ongoing pandemic recovery and reopening of in-person worship. This was a relatively stable period denominationally, though parishes nationwide were still managing the complex transition back to full congregational life after COVID-19 lockdowns.

DENVER & COLORADO NEWS

Denver was experiencing typical late summer weather with afternoon thunderstorm season active. The Colorado legislature had recently concluded its 2021 session in May, but local political attention was focused on the approaching 2021 municipal elections scheduled for November. Saint John's Cathedral, located in the Capitol Hill neighborhood, was serving a neighborhood experiencing ongoing gentrification pressures and discussions about affordable housing. The Denver Public Schools were in the early weeks of the 2021-22 school year, having navigated a contentious back-to-school period regarding mask mandates.

NATIONAL NEWS (US)

The dominant story remained the ongoing COVID-19 pandemic, with the Delta variant driving a surge in cases nationwide and vaccine mandate debates intensifying across corporate America and government. The Biden administration was in the midst of the Afghanistan withdrawal crisis, with the chaotic evacuation from Kabul having concluded on August 30, 2021, leaving behind deep political and national security questions. The commemoration of the 20th anniversary of 9/11 was approaching (September 11, 2021 had just occurred the day before this sermon), dominating news coverage and national reflection on security, faith, and American identity.

INTERNATIONAL NEWS

The Taliban had just completed their takeover of Afghanistan following the U.S. withdrawal, establishing an interim government and raising humanitarian concerns about the fate of Afghan civilians and interpreters left behind. The Western Balkans were experiencing renewed tensions as Serbia and Kosovo engaged in escalating rhetoric over mutual recognition and border disputes. Hurricane Ida had struck Louisiana on August 29, 2021, causing catastrophic damage and deaths, with cleanup and recovery dominating international disaster coverage.

CULTURAL MOMENT

The nation was in a reflective but anxious mood, marked by the 20th anniversary of 9/11 (September 11, 2021), which brought renewed discussions about national trauma, resilience, and the role of faith communities. The COVID-19 pandemic remained a source of deep social division, with vaccination status becoming a flashpoint for arguments about freedom, government authority, and public health. Popular culture was moving toward fall season programming, with streaming services and networks launching new content; the Denver Broncos were preparing for their NFL season opener on September 12, 2021, against the New York Giants, giving the congregation a local sports focal point as the city anticipated the football season.
